edges.description: Vessel fragility in CAA produces spontaneous lobar intracerebral hemorrhage.
Small-vessel hemorrhagic injury produces cortical and subcortical microbleeds.
CAA-related cortical vessel fragility can produce convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Chronic cortical bleeding leaves superficial hemosiderin deposition.
intermediate_mechanisms: Recurrent cortical or convexity subarachnoid bleeding with hemosiderin deposition.

edges.description: Cortical hemorrhagic lesions can trigger transient focal neurologic episodes.
intermediate_mechanisms: Convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage or cortical superficial siderosis.

edges.description: Non-hemorrhagic small-vessel injury and co-pathology contribute to progressive cognitive decline.
intermediate_mechanisms: Non-hemorrhagic brain injury from CAA small-vessel disease.

edges.description: Progressive CAA-related cognitive decline can culminate in dementia.
CAA-related inflammatory brain injury can manifest with seizures.
Non-hemorrhagic CAA small-vessel injury produces white matter hyperintensities on MRI.

nodes.description: Strictly lobar (cortical and subcortical) cerebral microbleeds are a core hemorrhagic neuroimaging marker of CAA; a higher number of strictly cortical microbleeds improves diagnostic specificity and predicts recurrent ICH risk.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Clinical Presentation, Sequelae and Neuroimaging Features-An Update.
evidence.snippet:
neuroradiologic findings of CAA include cortical and subcortical microbleeds 
(MB)

nodes.description: Cortical (convexity) subarachnoid hemorrhage is a characteristic hemorrhagic manifestation of CAA, often underlying transient focal neurological episodes.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Management of Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy.
evidence.snippet:
transient focal neurologic episodes attributed to convexity subarachnoid 
hemorrhage or cortical superficial siderosis, and progressive cognitive decline

nodes.description: Cortical superficial siderosis (cSS) reflects chronic blood-breakdown products over the cortical surface; disseminated cSS is among the strongest predictors of future intracerebral hemorrhage in CAA.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Clinical Presentation, Sequelae and Neuroimaging Features-An Update.
evidence.snippet: (MB), cortical subarachnoid hemorrhage (cSAH) and cortical superficial siderosis

nodes.description: Progressive cognitive decline in CAA can lead to dementia.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Management of Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy.
evidence.snippet:
progressive cognitive decline 
leading to dementia

nodes.description: Vessel fragility and small-vessel dysfunction produce both non-hemorrhagic injury (white matter hyperintensities, microinfarcts, enlarged perivascular spaces) and hemorrhagic lesions (strictly lobar cerebral microbleeds, convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage, cortical superficial siderosis, and lobar intracerebral hemorrhage), and contributes to cognitive decline. This is the final stage of the progression framework.

evidence.reference_title: Progression of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a pathophysiological framework.
evidence.snippet:
(stage four) 
appearance of haemorrhagic brain lesions

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Clinical Presentation, Sequelae and Neuroimaging Features-An Update.
evidence.snippet:
neuroradiologic findings of CAA include cortical and subcortical microbleeds 
(MB), cortical subarachnoid hemorrhage (cSAH) and cortical superficial siderosis

nodes.description: Exogenous Abeta assemblies introduced via contaminated cadaveric tissue can seed vascular and parenchymal Abeta pathology after long incubation periods, consistent with prion-like templated misfolding, producing acquired (iatrogenic) CAA and Alzheimer-type pathology decades after exposure.

evidence.reference_title: Iatrogenic Alzheimer's disease in recipients of cadaveric pituitary-derived growth hormone.
evidence.snippet:
We 
previously reported human transmission of Aβ pathology and CAA in relatively 
young adults who had died of iatrogenic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (iCJD) after 
childhood treatment with cadaver-derived pituitary growth hormone (c-hGH) 
contaminated with both CJD prions and Aβ seeds.

evidence.reference_title: Iatrogenic Alzheimer's disease in recipients of cadaveric pituitary-derived growth hormone.
evidence.snippet:
As propagating Aβ 
assemblies may exhibit structural diversity akin to conventional prions

nodes.description: Reduced clearance of soluble Abeta along perivascular (intramural periarterial) drainage pathways leads to its progressive accumulation in cortical and leptomeningeal vessel walls. APOE modulates Abeta processing, aggregation, and clearance, making APOE genotype the dominant common genetic determinant of sporadic CAA.

evidence.reference_title: Deciphering the role of APOE in cerebral amyloid angiopathy: from genetic insights to therapeutic horizons.
evidence.snippet: it plays a known role in processing, production, aggregation, and clearance

nodes.description: CAA is a major cause of spontaneous strictly lobar (cortical/subcortical) intracerebral hemorrhage, which has high mortality, morbidity, and a high recurrence rate.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a scientific statement from the International CAA Association and the World Stroke Organization.
evidence.snippet:
CAA is a major cause of spontaneous lobar intracerebral hemorrhage 
(ICH), and can also cause transient focal neurological episodes, and convexity 
subarachnoid hemorrhage, CAA-associated ICH has a high mortality, morbidity, and 
recurrence rate.

nodes.description: CAA contributes to vascular cognitive impairment and progressive cognitive decline that may lead to dementia, and frequently coexists with Alzheimer's disease pathology.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Clinical Presentation, Sequelae and Neuroimaging Features-An Update.
evidence.snippet:
transient focal neurologic episodes (TFNE) and progressive cognitive 
decline, potentially leading to Alzheimer's disease (AD)

nodes.description: Seizures occur particularly in CAA-related inflammation (CAA-ri), a treatable inflammatory subtype presenting with subacute neuropsychiatric and cognitive symptoms and asymmetric white matter lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy related inflammation: An under recognized but treatable complication of cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
evidence.snippet:
cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) causing a reversible encephalopathy 
characterized by seizures and focal neurological deficit.

nodes.description: Transient focal neurologic episodes (TFNE; "amyloid spells") are recurrent, often stereotyped, transient symptoms attributed to convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage or cortical superficial siderosis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Management of Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy.
evidence.snippet:
transient focal neurologic episodes attributed to convexity subarachnoid 
hemorrhage or cortical superficial siderosis

nodes.description: Aβ deposition in the walls of small and medium cortical and leptomeningeal vessels of the cerebrum and cerebellum is the defining lesion of CAA. This is the first stage of a multi-decade progression framework.

cell_types: Vascular smooth muscle cell of the brain vasculature

evidence.reference_title: Deciphering the role of APOE in cerebral amyloid angiopathy: from genetic insights to therapeutic horizons.
evidence.snippet:
characterized by the deposition of amyloid-β 
(Aβ) peptides in the walls of medium and small vessels of the brain and 
leptomeninges

evidence.reference_title: Progression of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a pathophysiological framework.

nodes.description: Progressive vascular amyloid deposition is accompanied by loss of vascular smooth muscle cells, vessel-wall thickening, and altered cerebrovascular physiology, weakening the vessel wall. Hypertension is a major non-genetic trigger that promotes vessel-wall weakening and hemorrhage. These changes progress through altered cerebrovascular physiology to non-hemorrhagic brain injury.

cell_types: Vascular smooth muscle cell of the brain vasculature

evidence.reference_title: Progression of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a pathophysiological framework.
evidence.snippet:
(stage two) 
alteration of cerebrovascular physiology, (stage three) non-haemorrhagic brain injury

nodes.description: Multiple hyperintense lesions on T2-weighted MRI (a multispot white matter hyperintensity pattern) and dilated centrum semiovale perivascular spaces are non-hemorrhagic neuroimaging markers of CAA incorporated into the Boston criteria v2.0.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Clinical Presentation, Sequelae and Neuroimaging Features-An Update.
evidence.snippet:
Non-hemorrhagic pathologies include dilated perivascular spaces in the 
centrum semiovale and multiple hyperintense lesions on T2-weighted magnetic 
resonance imaging (MRI).
